name: British vs American English — Grammatical Discrepancies
description: Complete cross-reference of real grammatical differences between British English (BrE) and American English (AmE). Use this alongside the main grammar databases to identify and practice the divergences between the two varieties. Sources: Cambridge Grammar, British Council LearnEnglish, Onestopenglish (Maxwell & Clandfield), Wikipedia (AmE/BrE grammatical differences), Pearson GSE, Azar-Hagen Grammar Series.

sources: Cambridge Dictionary — British and American Grammar
British Council LearnEnglish — B1-B2 British English and American English
Onestopenglish — Differences in American and British English Grammar (Maxwell & Clandfield)
Wikipedia — American and British English Grammatical Differences

items.point: Present perfect vs simple past with 'just', 'already', 'yet'
items.bre: Present perfect REQUIRED with just, already, yet for recent past events with present relevance.
items.ame: Simple past ACCEPTED (and common) with just, already, yet when the action is considered finished.
example_pair: BrE: I've just eaten lunch. / Have you done it yet? / I've already seen that film.
AmE: I just ate lunch. / Did you do it yet? / I already saw that film.
items.key_difference: This is the most frequent BrE/AmE grammar difference. AmE speakers use simple past ~3× more than BrE with these adverbs.

items.point: Present perfect for recent past with present result (without time markers)
items.bre: Present perfect preferred: 'I've lost my key' (still can't find it).
items.ame: Simple past accepted: 'I lost my key' (even if still missing).
example_pair: BrE: I've finished the report. Can you check it?
AmE: I finished the report. Can you check it?
items.key_difference: BrE distinguishes more clearly between past finished actions and present-relevant ones.

items.point: Past perfect usage frequency
items.bre: Past perfect used less commonly; simple past preferred for sequencing when context is clear.
items.ame: Past perfect used more frequently, especially to disambiguate sequence of past events.
example_pair: BrE: We watched the news, then the documentary.
AmE: We had watched the Sunday night, then Monday we watched the other game.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar notes AmE uses past perfect more even when sequence is clear from context.

items.point: 'Be going to' for giving street directions
items.bre: Imperative or present simple used: 'Take this street, then go two hundred yards.'
items.ame: Be going to common (informal gonna): 'You're gonna go three blocks, then you're gonna see...'
example_pair: BrE: Turn left at the traffic lights, then go straight on.
AmE: You're gonna turn left at the traffic lights, then you're gonna go straight.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar specifically highlights this difference in AmE spoken directions.

items.point: Past participle of get: got vs gotten
items.bre: Past participle is got: 'I've got a cold' (have/possess), 'He's got thin' (become).
items.ame: Past participle is gotten when meaning 'obtained/become': 'I've gotten a cold', 'He's gotten thin'. HAVE + got is still used for possession/obligation (NOT have gotten).
example_pair: BrE: I've got a new car. (I have) / He's got angry quickly. (become)
AmE: I've gotten a new car. (obtained) / He's gotten angry. (become) / I've got two sisters. (have — same as BrE)
items.key_difference: Gotten is the ORIGINAL English form that BrE lost but AmE preserved. Both use 'have got' for possession.

items.point: Past tense of burn: burnt vs burned
items.bre: Both burnt and burned accepted; burnt is more common.
items.ame: Burned is strongly preferred; burnt is rare.
example_pair: BrE: The toast burnt. / She burnt the papers.
AmE: The toast burned. / She burned the papers.
items.key_difference: One of ~15 verbs where BrE accepts -t forms, AmE prefers -ed. See full list below.

items.point: Past tense of dream: dreamt vs dreamed
items.bre: Both dreamt and dreamed accepted; dreamt is common.
items.ame: Dreamed is strongly preferred; dreamt is very rare.
example_pair: BrE: I dreamt about you last night.
AmE: I dreamed about you last night.
items.key_difference: Same -t vs -ed pattern as burn, learn, etc.

items.point: Past tense of learn: learnt vs learned
items.bre: Both learnt and learned accepted; learnt is common.
items.ame: Learned is the ONLY standard form (learnt is rare/odd).
example_pair: BrE: I learnt French at school.
AmE: I learned French in school.
items.key_difference: This is the most noticeable -t vs -ed difference. Learned is also the spelling in both varieties when used as an adjective (a learned professor, /lɜːrnɪd/).

items.point: Past tense of spell: spelt vs spelled
items.bre: Both spelt and spelled accepted.
items.ame: Spelled is the ONLY standard form.
example_pair: BrE: She spelt my name wrong.
AmE: She spelled my name wrong.

items.point: Past tense of spill: spilt vs spilled
items.bre: Both spilt and spilled accepted.
items.ame: Spilled is strongly preferred.
example_pair: BrE: I spilt coffee on my shirt.
AmE: I spilled coffee on my shirt.

items.point: Past tense of spoil: spoilt vs spoiled
items.bre: Both spoilt and spoiled accepted.
items.ame: Spoiled is strongly preferred.
example_pair: BrE: The children were spoilt by their grandparents.
AmE: The children were spoiled by their grandparents.

items.point: Past tense of smell: smelt vs smelled
items.bre: Both smelt and smelled accepted.
items.ame: Smelled is the ONLY standard form.
example_pair: BrE: It smelt like roses.
AmE: It smelled like roses.

items.point: Past tense of lean: leant vs leaned
items.bre: Both leant and leaned accepted.
items.ame: Leaned is the ONLY standard form.
example_pair: BrE: He leant against the wall.
AmE: He leaned against the wall.

items.point: Past tense of dive: dived vs dove
items.bre: Dived is the ONLY standard form.
items.ame: Dove is the common past simple (dived also accepted).
example_pair: BrE: She dived into the pool.
AmE: She dove into the pool.
items.key_difference: One of the few cases where AmE uses an irregular form and BrE uses a regular one.

items.point: Past tense of fit: fitted vs fit
items.bre: Fitted is the standard past tense: 'The shirt fitted him.'
items.ame: Fit is used as past tense: 'The shirt fit him.'
example_pair: BrE: The suit fitted perfectly.
AmE: The suit fit perfectly.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar specifically notes: BrE usually fitted, AmE usually fit.

items.point: Past participle of prove: proved vs proven
items.bre: Proved is the standard past participle (proven used as adjective).
items.ame: Both proved and proven accepted as past participle; proven is very common.
example_pair: BrE: The theory has proved correct.
AmE: The theory has proven correct. / The theory has proved correct.
items.key_difference: Proven is more established in AmE as a past participle. In BrE it's mainly an adjective (a proven fact).

items.point: Past tense of bust: bust vs busted
items.bre: Bust is the standard past form.
items.ame: Busted is common as past tense.
example_pair: BrE: He bust the lock.
AmE: He busted the lock.
items.key_difference: Onestopenglish lists busted as distinctly AmE.

items.point: Past participle of saw: sawn vs sawed
items.bre: Sawn is the standard past participle: 'The wood was sawn in half.'
items.ame: Sawed is common: 'The wood was sawed in half.'
example_pair: BrE: He has sawn through the log.
AmE: He has sawed through the log.

items.point: Past tense of plead: pleaded vs pled
items.bre: Pleaded is standard; pled is rare/legal jargon.
items.ame: Both pleaded and pled accepted; pled is common especially in legal contexts.
example_pair: BrE: He pleaded guilty.
AmE: He pled guilty. / He pleaded guilty.

items.point: Past tense of stink: stank vs stunk
items.bre: Stank is the standard past simple; stunk as past participle.
items.ame: Both stank and stunk accepted as past simple.
example_pair: BrE: The room stank of smoke.
AmE: The room stunk. / The room stank.

items.point: Past tense of wake: woke vs waked
items.bre: Woke is the only standard past tense.
items.ame: Both woke and waked accepted; waked especially in formal/nautical use.
example_pair: BrE: I woke up at 7.
AmE: I woke up at 7. / I waked up early.

items.point: Collective nouns: singular vs plural verb (team, committee, government, family, staff)
items.bre: Both SINGULAR and PLURAL verbs accepted. Singular = unit; Plural = individuals. 'The team is playing well' / 'The team are arguing.'
items.ame: SINGULAR verb REQUIRED with collective nouns. 'The team is playing well' / 'The team IS arguing' (NOT 'are').
example_pair: BrE: My family is/are visiting from Pakistan. / The government have announced...
AmE: My family is visiting from Pakistan. / The government has announced...
items.key_difference: This is the most famous BrE/AmE grammar difference after present perfect. BrE uses 'notional agreement' (meaning-based); AmE uses 'formal agreement' (form-based). Exception in both: 'police' always takes plural.

items.point: Sports team names: singular vs plural verb
items.bre: Singular verb if team name is singular: 'FC Red Bull Salzburg IS an Austrian club.'
items.ame: Plural verb often used even with singular team names: 'The New York Red Bulls ARE an American team.'
example_pair: BrE: Arsenal is playing tomorrow. / Liverpool are winning.
AmE: The Lakers are playing tonight. / The Yankees are winning.
items.key_difference: Wikipedia notes this is an area of systematic divergence. AmE treats team names as plural collectives regardless of form.

items.point: The United States: singular vs earlier plural agreement
items.bre: Treated as singular (same as AmE): 'The United States has decided...'
items.ame: STRICTLY singular after the Civil War: 'The United States is...'
example_pair: BrE: The United States has a large economy.
AmE: The United States is a democracy. (usage standardised to singular post-1865)
items.key_difference: Before the Civil War, 'The United States are...' was common. Now universally singular in both varieties.

items.point: Have got vs have for possession and relationships
items.bre: Have got is VERY common in spoken BrE for possession/relationships: 'I've got two cousins in Ohio.'
items.ame: Have (without got) is preferred: 'I have two cousins in Ohio.' ('I've got' is understood but less common.)
example_pair: BrE: I've got a car. / Have you got any money? / She's got blue eyes.
AmE: I have a car. / Do you have any money? / She has blue eyes.
items.key_difference: AmE: 'I have' is default. BrE: 'I've got' is default in speech. Cambridge Grammar calls this a key difference.

items.point: Have got to vs have to for obligation
items.bre: Have got to is more common in spoken BrE for obligation.
items.ame: Have to is preferred; have got to is less common.
example_pair: BrE: I've got to take my mother back to hospital.
AmE: I have to be back in San Francisco next Sunday.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: BrE uses have got to more; AmE prefers have to.

items.point: Have as auxiliary in questions: 'Have you...?' vs 'Do you have...?'
items.bre: Auxiliary have in questions is common: 'Have you any money?', 'Have you a moment?'
items.ame: Do-support required: 'Do you have any money?', 'Do you have a moment?'
example_pair: BrE: Have you a pen? / I haven't a clue.
AmE: Do you have a pen? / I don't have a clue.
items.key_difference: AmE treats have as a main verb requiring do-support. BrE can use it as an auxiliary (like have got).

items.point: Shall for first-person future
items.bre: Shall is traditionally used with I/we for future (formal): 'I shall return.' Still used in formal/educated speech.
items.ame: Will is used for all persons. Shall is very rare outside legal/quotations. 'I will return.'
example_pair: BrE: I shall be back in a minute. / We shall overcome.
AmE: I'll call you tomorrow. / We will overcome.
items.key_difference: Shall is dying even in BrE but still exists in formal contexts. Almost extinct in AmE.

items.point: Shall I/we for offers and suggestions vs Can/Should/How about
items.bre: Common use of 'Shall I...?' for offers and 'Shall we...?' for suggestions.
items.ame: Alternatives used: 'Should/Can I...?' for offers; 'Do you want to...?' or 'How about...' for suggestions.
example_pair: BrE: Shall I open the window? / Shall we meet at 5?
AmE: Should I open the window? / Do you want to meet at 5?
items.key_difference: British Council lists this as one of the 5 key grammatical differences. AmE speakers rarely use shall for offers/suggestions.

items.point: Needn't vs don't need to for lack of necessity
items.bre: Needn't (modal form) is common especially in BrE: 'You needn't come if you're busy.'
items.ame: Don't need to (regular verb form) is standard: 'You don't need to come if you're busy.' Needn't is very unusual in AmE.
example_pair: BrE: You needn't wait. / Need I say more?
AmE: You don't need to wait. / Do I need to say more?
items.key_difference: Onestopenglish: 'Needn't is very unusual in AmE and the usual form is don't need to.'

items.point: Do as verb substitute after modals
items.bre: Do is added after modals in short answers: '—Will you come? —I might do.' '—Should I go? —You should do.'
items.ame: Modal used alone without do: '—Will you come? —I might.' '—Should I go? —You should.'
example_pair: BrE: I might do, but I doubt it.
AmE: Oh, I might.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: BrE adds do after modals; AmE uses modal alone.

items.point: Dare: modal vs regular verb usage
items.bre: Dare can be used as a modal (no -s, no do-support): 'I daren't ask.', 'Dare he go?'
items.ame: Dare is almost always a regular verb: 'I don't dare to ask.' 'Does he dare to go?'
example_pair: BrE: I daren't tell him. / How dare you! / Dare she go?
AmE: I don't dare (to) tell him. / How dare you! (fixed expression) / Does she dare to go?
items.key_difference: Modal dare is mainly BrE. In AmE, 'How dare you!' is a fixed expression; all other uses are regular.

items.point: Preposition with weekend: at vs on
items.bre: at the weekend: 'What are you doing at the weekend?'
items.ame: on the weekend: 'What are you doing on the weekend?'
example_pair: BrE: We'll go to the beach at the weekend.
AmE: We'll go to the beach on the weekend.
items.key_difference: This is one of the most common preposition differences. Cambridge Grammar explicitly contrasts these.

items.point: Preposition with streets: in vs on
items.bre: in + street: 'She lives in Oxford Street.'
items.ame: on + street: 'She lives on Main Street.'
example_pair: BrE: The shop is in High Street.
AmE: The store is on Main Street.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: BrE in, AmE on for street names.

items.point: Different to/from vs different from/than
items.bre: different TO or FROM: 'This is different to what I expected.' / 'Different from' is also standard.
items.ame: different FROM or THAN: 'This is different than what I expected.' / 'Different from' is standard in formal writing.
example_pair: BrE: This is different to the original.
AmE: This is different than the original.
items.key_difference: Both varieties accept 'different from'. 'Different to' is distinctly BrE. 'Different than' is distinctly AmE.

items.point: Write to someone vs write someone (omission of to)
items.bre: to is required: 'I'll write to you tomorrow.'
items.ame: to can be omitted: 'I'll write you tomorrow.' (write + direct object)
example_pair: BrE: I wrote to her last week.
AmE: I wrote her last week.
items.key_difference: Onestopenglish: AmE can omit to; BrE requires it.

items.point: Monday to Friday vs Monday through Friday
items.bre: to/till for time ranges: 'Monday to Friday', '9 to 5'.
items.ame: through for inclusive ranges: 'Monday through Friday', '9 to 5' also used.
example_pair: BrE: The shop is open Monday to Friday.
AmE: The store is open Monday through Friday.
items.key_difference: Through is distinctly AmE for inclusive ranges. Cambridge Grammar notes this.

items.point: For vs in with negative time expressions (haven't done X in/for Y years)
items.bre: for is standard: 'I haven't seen him for years.'
items.ame: in is common: 'I haven't seen him in years.' (Both for and in used.)
example_pair: BrE: I haven't had a holiday for three years.
AmE: I haven't had a holiday in three years.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: AmE uses in where BrE uses for in negative time expressions.

items.point: At university/in school vs in university/at school
items.bre: at university: 'She's studying at university.'
items.ame: in university OR in college: 'She's studying in college.' (Also 'at college'.)
example_pair: BrE: He's at university studying law.
AmE: He's in college studying law.
items.key_difference: Onestopenglish: BrE 'at university'; AmE 'in university/college'. Also 'go to university' vs 'go to college'.

items.point: Cater for vs cater to
items.bre: cater FOR: 'The hotel caters for all dietary requirements.'
items.ame: cater TO: 'The hotel caters to all dietary needs.'
example_pair: BrE: This school caters for children with special needs.
AmE: This school caters to children with special needs.

items.point: Protest vs protest against
items.bre: protest AGAINST: 'Thousands protested against the decision.'
items.ame: protest (transitive, no against): 'Thousands protested the decision.'
example_pair: BrE: Workers protested against low wages.
AmE: Workers protested low wages.
items.key_difference: AmE treats protest as a transitive verb; BrE requires a preposition.

items.point: Meet vs meet with
items.bre: meet (transitive) for people: 'I'll meet you at the station.' (Also 'meet with' but less common.)
items.ame: meet WITH for arranged meetings: 'I'll meet with the client tomorrow.' (meet = first time encounter.)
example_pair: BrE: I'm meeting the manager at 3.
AmE: I'm meeting with the manager at 3.
items.key_difference: In AmE, 'meet with' = scheduled meeting; 'meet' = first encounter. In BrE, 'meet' covers both.

items.point: Definite article with hospitals and institutions: in hospital vs in the hospital
items.bre: NO article for institutional meaning: 'He's in hospital' (as patient), 'She's at university' (as student). Article used for building: 'The hospital is on Main Street.'
items.ame: THE required: 'He's in THE hospital' (patient), 'She's at THE university' (student). Abstract use without article is limited to school, church, prison, college.
example_pair: BrE: She's in hospital recovering from surgery.
AmE: She's in the hospital recovering from surgery.
items.key_difference: BrE omits the article for institutions when referring to their primary function (hospital→treatment, university→study). AmE includes the.

items.point: Go to hospital vs go to the hospital
items.bre: go to hospital (no article): 'He went to hospital for an operation.'
items.ame: go to THE hospital: 'He went to the hospital for surgery.'
example_pair: BrE: I had to go to hospital last night.
AmE: I had to go to the hospital last night.
items.key_difference: Same pattern as BA-043. BrE treats these as 'bare institutional nouns'.

items.point: At (the) front/back: article with positions
items.bre: In cars: 'at the front' / 'at the back'. Also 'in the front'/'in the back'.
items.ame: In cars: 'in the front' / 'in the back'. 'I sat in the back of the car.'
example_pair: BrE: The driver sits at the front of the car.
AmE: The driver sits in the front of the car.
items.key_difference: BrE uses at for vehicle positions; AmE uses in.

items.point: Have a bath/shower/wash/rest/break vs take a bath/shower/wash/rest/break
items.bre: HAVE is the default delexical verb: 'I'm going to have a shower.', 'Let's have a break.' (Take also used but less common.)
items.ame: TAKE is the default: 'I'm going to take a shower.', 'Let's take a break.' (Have in this context is not used.)
example_pair: BrE: I always have a shower in the morning. / Let's have a rest.
AmE: I always take a shower in the morning. / Let's take a rest.
items.key_difference: British Council: AmE only uses take. BrE uses have primarily but take is also understood. This is one of the 5 key BrE/AmE differences.

items.point: Have a holiday vs take a vacation
items.bre: HAVE a holiday: 'Where are you going for your holiday?'
items.ame: TAKE a vacation: 'Where are you going on vacation?'
example_pair: BrE: We're having a holiday in Spain.
AmE: We're taking a vacation to Spain.
items.key_difference: Both vocabulary (holiday vs vacation) and verb choice (have vs take) differ.

items.point: Mandative subjunctive after suggest/recommend/insist/demand
items.bre: Should + infinitive OR indicative preferred: 'I suggest that he should go.' / 'I demanded that he was present.' (Subjunctive also used but less common, increasing due to AmE influence.)
items.ame: Present subjunctive REQUIRED: 'I suggest that he go.' / 'I demanded that he BE present.' (Subjunctive strongly maintained.)
example_pair: BrE: It is essential that everyone should be informed. / The doctor recommended that he takes more exercise.
AmE: It is essential that everyone be informed. / The doctor recommended that he take more exercise.
items.key_difference: Academic research shows AmE is leading a 'subjunctive revival'. BrE has traditionally preferred should-periphrasis or indicative. This difference is narrowing due to AmE influence.

items.point: Mandative subjunctive after 'importance' adjectives (essential, vital, necessary)
items.bre: Should is preferred: 'It's vital that he should attend.'
items.ame: Subjunctive is standard: 'It's vital that he attend.'
example_pair: BrE: It's important that everyone should understand.
AmE: It's important that everyone understand.

items.point: Subjunctive with request/demand nouns: 'a suggestion that...'
items.bre: Should preferred: 'The suggestion that he should resign was rejected.'
items.ame: Subjunctive: 'The suggestion that he resign was rejected.'
example_pair: BrE: Our recommendation that the project should be funded was approved.
AmE: Our recommendation that the project be funded was approved.

items.point: Were-subjunctive in fixed phrases and conditionals
items.bre: Used similarly to AmE: 'If I were you...', 'I wish I were...'. However, 'I wish I was...' is also very common in informal BrE.
items.ame: Were-subjunctive preserved: 'If I were you...', 'I wish I were...'. 'Was' in these contexts is considered non-standard.
example_pair: BrE: If I were/was you, I'd accept. / I wish I was/were taller.
AmE: If I were you, I'd accept. / I wish I were taller.
items.key_difference: AmE is stricter about were-subjunctive. BrE accepts was increasingly in informal contexts.

items.point: Real vs really as adverb
items.bre: Really is the ONLY standard adverb: 'That's really funny.' (Real as adverb is non-standard.)
items.ame: Real is used informally as an adverb: 'That's real funny.' (Really is standard in formal contexts.)
example_pair: BrE: That's really good. / She's really nice.
AmE: That's real good. / She's real nice.
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: AmE informal uses real as adverb; this is non-standard in BrE.

items.point: Good vs well after copular verbs (I'm good vs I'm well)
items.bre: I'm well/fine (standard). I'm good is used but originally an Americanism now spreading.
items.ame: I'm good is the STANDARD response: 'How are you? —I'm good.'
example_pair: BrE: 'How are you?' — 'I'm well, thanks.'
AmE: 'How are you?' — 'I'm good.'
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: I'm good is distinctly AmE, though spreading in BrE. 'It worked out real good' has both adverbs (real + good) and is distinctly AmE.

items.point: Likely as adverb vs adjective
items.bre: Likely is normally an ADJECTIVE: 'There are likely to be other announcements.' NOT used as adverb: *'likely will'.
items.ame: Likely can be used as ADVERB (=probably): 'There will likely be other announcements.', 'The focus will likely continue.'
example_pair: BrE: It is likely to rain later. (adjective)
AmE: It will likely rain later. (adverb)
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: AmE uses likely as a sentence adverb (like probably). BrE restricts it to adjective use.

items.point: Adverb position with yet in questions
items.bre: Yet at END position: 'Have you finished yet?'
items.ame: Yet can appear in MID position: 'Have you yet finished?' (formal) or simple past + end yet: 'Did you finish yet?'
example_pair: BrE: Have you seen the film yet?
AmE: Did you see the film yet?
items.key_difference: Related to BA-001 (present perfect vs past simple). The word order difference is driven by tense choice.

items.point: Frequency and variety of question tags
items.bre: Question tags are VERY FREQUENT with a wide variety: 'isn't it?', 'aren't they?', 'won't you?', 'shall we?', 'will you?', 'can you?', 'would you?'.
items.ame: Question tags are LESS FREQUENT. Alternatives like 'right?', 'huh?', 'okay?', 'don't you think?' are more common in informal speech.
example_pair: BrE: Open the door, will you? / You're coming, aren't you? / Let's go, shall we?
AmE: Open the door, okay? / You're coming, right? / Let's go, huh?
items.key_difference: Research (Tottie & Hoffmann) shows AmE uses more 'facilitating' tags (right? okay?), while BrE uses more canonical tags. But the overall tag rate is still similar.

items.point: Same-form tags with rising intonation (expressive tags)
items.bre: Pattern: Auxiliary ± n't + subject pronoun. Rising intonation shows surprise but less common than in AmE.
items.ame: Same-form tag (affirmative + affirmative) with rising intonation to show surprise: 'Oh, you did↗?' 'You are↗?'
example_pair: BrE: Oh, did you? (standard question tag)
AmE: Oh, you did↗? (same-form tag with rising intonation)
items.key_difference: Cambridge Grammar: AmE uses same-form tags with rising intonation to show surprise; BrE uses standard question tags.

items.point: One/one's vs his/her for impersonal reference
items.bre: One + one's is maintained: 'One should do one's best.'
items.ame: One + his/her or they/their: 'One should do his best.' Or using 'you/your': 'You should do your best.'
example_pair: BrE: One must always keep one's promises.
AmE: One must always keep his promises. / You should always keep your promises.
items.key_difference: BrE preserves the reflexive one → one's pattern better. AmE finds it awkward and substitutes his/their/your.

items.point: -ise vs -ize verb endings (organise/organize)
items.bre: Both accepted: organise (traditional) and organize (Oxford spelling). Newspapers prefer -ise; academic prefers -ize.
items.ame: ONLY -ize: organize, realize, recognize, apologize. -ise is a spelling error.
example_pair: BrE: The company is organised/organized.
AmE: The company is organized.
items.key_difference: Note: -ize forms originated in English (Greek -izo) and are NOT an American invention. Both are correct in BrE; only -ize in AmE.

items.point: -our vs -or noun endings (colour/color, favour/favor)
items.bre: -our: colour, favour, honour, behaviour, neighbour.
items.ame: -or: color, favor, honor, behavior, neighbor.
example_pair: BrE: What colour is your favourite?
AmE: What color is your favorite?
items.key_difference: Spelling-only difference but affects written grammar in terms of adjective/participle forms (colourful/colorful).

items.point: -re vs -er endings (centre/center, theatre/theater)
items.bre: -re: centre, theatre, metre, litre, fibre.
items.ame: -er: center, theater, meter, liter, fiber.
example_pair: BrE: The theatre is in the city centre.
AmE: The theater is in the city center.
items.key_difference: Spelling difference. Note: 'meter' in BrE is a measuring device; 'metre' is the unit of length.

items.point: -l- vs -ll- in past forms (traveled/travelled, canceled/cancelled)
items.bre: DOUBLE -ll- before -ed/-ing/-er: travelled, travelling, traveller, cancelled, labelling.
items.ame: SINGLE -l-: traveled, traveling, traveler, canceled, labeling. (Exception: accent falls on second syllable → AmE also doubles: 'compelled', 'excelled'.)
example_pair: BrE: I travelled to London. / The flight was cancelled.
AmE: I traveled to London. / The flight was canceled.
items.key_difference: This is a systematic spelling difference that affects verb conjugation in written grammar.

items.point: Past tense with -ed vs -t (complete verb list: burn, dream, learn, spell, spill, spoil, smell, leap, lean)
items.bre: Both -ed and -t forms accepted for ALL of these verbs; -t forms are common.
items.ame: -ed forms are the STANDARD for ALL of these verbs; -t forms are rare or archaic.
example_pair: BrE: He dreamt of flying. / She learnt Spanish. / It smelt strange.
AmE: He dreamed of flying. / She learned Spanish. / It smelled strange.
items.key_difference: This group of ~15 verbs systematically differs. The -t forms are historically older; AmE regularised them.

items.point: Haven't (got) any vs don't have any — negation of possession
items.bre: I haven't any. / I haven't got any. (both common; the latter more common in speech)
items.ame: I don't have any. (I haven't got any is understood but sounds British.)
example_pair: BrE: I haven't any money. / I haven't got any money.
AmE: I don't have any money.

items.point: Get + object + to-infinitive vs get + object + past participle
items.bre: Get sb to do: 'I'll get him to call you back.' / Get sth done: 'I'll get it fixed.'
items.ame: Same patterns, but 'get + object + past participle' is MORE COMMON in AmE: 'I got my car repaired.' (Both varieties use have/get causative.)
example_pair: BrE: I'll get him to fix my car.
AmE: I'll get my car fixed.
items.key_difference: Minor frequency difference in causative patterns. Both varieties use both forms.

items.point: Preposition stranding vs pied-piping in relative clauses
items.bre: Pied-piping (preposition before relative pronoun) is more common in formal BrE: 'The man to whom I spoke...'
items.ame: Preposition stranding is more accepted: 'The man who I spoke to...' Both use stranding in speech, but AmE accepts it more freely in writing.
example_pair: BrE (formal): The person with whom you spoke. (informal: The person you spoke to.)
AmE: The person you spoke to. (formal also: The person with whom you spoke.)
items.key_difference: Subtle register difference. BrE maintains pied-piping in formal contexts more strictly. Both use stranding informally.

items.point: 'There you go' vs 'There you are' for handing something
items.bre: Both used: 'There you are' and 'There you go' when giving something to someone.
items.ame: 'There you go' is the default. 'There you are' is less common.
example_pair: BrE: 'Here's your coffee. There you are.'
AmE: 'Here's your coffee. There you go.'

items.point: Use of 'lovely' as all-purpose positive adjective
items.bre: Lovely is used very frequently and broadly: 'lovely weather', 'That's lovely!', 'Have a lovely day!'
items.ame: Lovely is less common; 'nice', 'great', 'beautiful' are preferred.
example_pair: BrE: We had a lovely time. / What a lovely idea!
AmE: We had a great time. / What a nice idea!
items.key_difference: More of a pragmatic/discourse difference. The overuse of 'lovely' is a known BrE marker.

items.point: Past tense for politeness/distancing (I was wondering, I thought)
items.bre: Very common for politeness: 'I was wondering if you could help.' 'I thought I might ask.' (Unreal past for distancing.)
items.ame: Also used but slightly less frequent. Simple present more common: 'I wonder if you can help.'
example_pair: BrE: I was wondering if you could spare a moment.
AmE: I wonder if you can help me for a minute.
items.key_difference: Subtle frequency difference. BrE uses more complex distancing patterns.
